synopsis: Alasdair Nairn traces the financial history of transformative technologies—from railroads and electricity to the telephone, computer, and internet—revealing that major innovations follow recurring patterns of investor skepticism, speculative enthusiasm, and eventual market consolidation. By studying what these cycles meant for investors across two centuries, the book offers a framework for evaluating emerging technology companies.

sources.title: Engines That Move Markets – Pan Macmillan

synopsis: Andrew Howell and David York outline a multi-generational approach to wealth transfer called Entrusted Planning, which aligns a family's values and principles with its assets rather than simply transferring money. The framework rests on seven disciplines shared by durable high-net-worth families, including preparing beneficiaries for stewardship and building dynamic governance structures.

sources.title: Entrusted: Building a Legacy That Lasts – Amazon

synopsis: Edward Tufte examines how complex, high-dimensional information can be displayed effectively across maps, charts, statistical graphics, timetables, and diagrams, drawing on hundreds of historical and contemporary examples. The book develops principles such as small multiples, layering, and the use of color to help designers escape the limitations of flat two-dimensional surfaces.

John Maynard Keynes collected his public essays and articles from 1919 to 1940 in an attempt to shape economic and political events through persuasion, covering topics from the Versailles peace terms and reparations to inflation, the gold standard, and the prospects of capitalism. The volume shows how Keynes's arguments about monetary policy and aggregate demand developed in the years before his major theoretical works.

Riley Foster walks through the core instruments of estate planning—living trusts, powers of attorney, and beneficiary designations—and explains how to structure them to transfer wealth while minimizing taxes and avoiding probate. The book covers four broad areas: estate planning essentials, crafting an estate plan, tax strategies, and the role of life insurance.

Set in the fictional post-apocalyptic city-state of Eumeswil, Ernst Jünger's novel follows Manuel Venator, a historian who tends bar for the ruling dictator while secretly recording overheard conversations and reflections in a private journal. Through Venator, Jünger develops his concept of the anarch—an individual who preserves inner freedom and autonomy while living under authoritarian rule.

Kevin Kelly compiled 450 aphorisms he wrote starting on his 68th birthday as advice he wished he had known earlier, covering relationships, creativity, decision-making, and generosity. The pieces are short, tweet-length observations offering practical guidance drawn from Kelly's experience as a technologist, writer, and co-founder of Wired magazine.

sources.title: Excellent Advice for Living – Penguin Random House

synopsis: Ted Chiang's second collection gathers nine science fiction stories that explore free will, consciousness, the ethics of technology, and the relationship between humans and artificial minds. The stories range from an ancient Baghdad tale involving time travel to a title piece in which a mechanical being dissects his own brain to understand the nature of existence.

Antti Ilmanen provides a systematic analysis of the expected returns available across major asset classes—equities, bonds, credit, real assets, and alternative strategies—examining the historical evidence, theoretical rationale, and current conditions for each. The book addresses how growth, inflation, liquidity, and behavioral factors such as extrapolation and overconfidence drive the risk premiums investors can harvest.

Jocko Willink and Leif Babin draw on their combat experience commanding SEAL Task Unit Bruiser in Ramadi, Iraq, to articulate twelve leadership principles organized around the idea that a leader must take full personal responsibility for every outcome of the team. Each chapter pairs a battlefield story with an explanation of the underlying principle and a parallel case study from the business world.

Steven Levy traces Facebook's history from Mark Zuckerberg's Harvard dorm room to its position as a global social media platform, drawing on hundreds of interviews including multiple sessions with Zuckerberg himself. The account examines the company's growth-at-all-costs culture, its acquisitions of Instagram and WhatsApp, and the controversies over privacy, data sharing, misinformation, and foreign interference.

sources.title: Facebook: The Inside Story – Penguin Random House

synopsis: Hans Rosling, Ola Rosling, and Anna Rosling Rönnlund identify ten cognitive instincts—including the gap instinct, the fear instinct, and the straight-line instinct—that cause people to systematically misread global trends in health, poverty, education, and population. The book argues that a fact-based worldview, rather than dramatic narratives, reveals a world that has improved substantially across most measurable dimensions.

Ray Bradbury's dystopian novel is set in a future America where firemen burn books rather than fight fires, following Guy Montag, a fireman who begins to question the society that outlaws reading after encounters with a curious neighbor and a woman who burns with her library. The book examines censorship, conformity, and the dangers of replacing independent thought with passive mass entertainment.

Fridtjof Nansen recounts the 1893–1896 expedition of the specially built ship Fram, which was frozen into the Arctic ice pack to drift northward while Nansen and Lieutenant Johansen then attempted a sledge journey to reach the highest latitude ever achieved by Westerners. Volume I covers the planning and departure of the voyage and the early months locked in the polar ice.

sources.title: Farthest North Vol. I – Project Gutenberg

synopsis: David Burns applies cognitive behavioral therapy to depression, explaining that moods are generated by thoughts and that identifying and correcting distorted thinking patterns—such as overgeneralization, all-or-nothing thinking, and catastrophizing—can relieve depressive symptoms without medication. The book provides worksheets, case studies, and step-by-step exercises designed for readers to use on their own.

sources.title: Feeling Good: The New Mood Therapy – Amazon

synopsis: David Burns presents an updated cognitive behavioral approach that extends beyond depression to treat anxiety, arguing that negative feelings arise not from brain chemistry but from distorted thoughts rooted in a person's core values. Drawing on forty years of clinical practice, the book offers more than fifty tools for challenging ten categories of cognitive distortions that undermine mood and self-esteem.

Simon Singh chronicles the 350-year quest to prove Fermat's Last Theorem—Pierre de Fermat's 17th-century claim that the equation x^n + y^n = z^n has no whole-number solutions for n greater than 2—tracing the lives of mathematicians who devoted careers to the problem. The book culminates with Andrew Wiles's seven-year secret effort at Princeton that produced a complete proof in 1994.

Philosopher Barry Lam argues that the proliferation of mandatory rules—in law enforcement, sentencing, sports officiating, and other domains—has not eliminated bias but instead corrupted institutions by removing the moral judgment that effective governance requires. Lam proposes that organizations should cultivate trained decision-makers who exercise ethical discretion, and warns that AI-enforced rule systems will intensify the problem.

sources.title: Fewer Rules, Better People – Barnes & Noble

synopsis: These lectures, delivered by Richard Feynman at Caltech in 1962–63, develop a quantum field theory approach to gravitation that treats gravity as a massless spin-2 field rather than beginning from the geometric language of general relativity. Feynman uses this framework to derive Einstein's field equations and to explore topics including cosmology, gravitational waves, and the connection between gauge invariance and the equivalence principle.
